serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw oyster mushroom is approximately 86 g (about 1 cup sliced), providing roughly 28 kcal, 2.8 g protein, and 5.2 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster mushroom, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14030005 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Mushrooms, oyster, raw (FDC ID 169403) where available; fields not available in either source are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms — analogous estimate based on non-starchy fungal vegetables; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on the published GI of non-starchy mushrooms and fungal vegetables (GI range 10–20) as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 5.24) / 100 ≈ 0.8, where carbohydrate per 86 g serving = 6.09 × 0.86 = 5.24 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (18 mg/100 g) and high potassium (420 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Oyster mushrooms are an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals: their sodium content of 18 mg/100 g is negligible and their potassium content of 420 mg/100 g actively supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~15), low carbohydrate (6.1 g/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g), and beta-glucan content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of approximately 15, only 6.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g, and 2.3 g dietary fibre including beta-glucans that slow glucose absorption, oyster mushrooms are a low-glycaemic food that supports postprandial blood glucose control in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.06 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and beta-glucan soluble fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oyster mushrooms are cardioprotective: saturated fat is negligible at 0.06 g/100 g, cholesterol is 0 mg, and their beta-glucan content has been associated with LDL cholesterol reduction; their high potassium further supports cardiovascular health by moderating blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan immunomodulatory activity, low saturated fat, and antioxidant micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oyster mushrooms contain beta-glucans that modulate immune response relevant to RA management; they are low in saturated fat and provide iron and B-vitamins as antioxidant cofactors — best consumed steamed or stir-fried to preserve beta-glucan integrity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus), consumed raw, is a widely cultivated edible fungus recognised by its grey-brown, fan-shaped or oyster-shaped caps with a firm yet tender texture and a mild, earthy, slightly sweet flavour. It is one of the most commercially important mushrooms in East and Southeast Asia and is used extensively in Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Nutritionally, per 100 g raw oyster mushroom provides approximately 33 kcal, 3.3 g protein, 0.4 g fat (of which only 0.06 g is saturated), and 6.1 g carbohydrate with 2.3 g dietary fibre. It is notably rich in potassium (420 mg), B-vitamins including niacin (4.96 mg), riboflavin (0.35 mg), and folate (38 mcg), and provides a small but meaningful amount of vitamin D (0.7 mcg) and iron (1.33 mg). Sodium is very low at 18 mg/100 g. The mushroom is a source of beta-glucans, a class of soluble polysaccharides with documented immunomodulatory and cholesterol-lowering properties. Its estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Oyster mushrooms are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, they are appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. They are best consumed lightly cooked — stir-fried, steamed, or sautéed — to preserve their nutritional and bioactive content, though raw forms are also used in salads.
common_user_queries: Is oyster mushroom good for diabetes?
Is oyster mushroom good for blood pressure?

ingredient_synonyms.fan mushroom: descriptive English variant based on cap shape

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14030005, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid sub-fractions are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403), an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct measurement exists; this is documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.06 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.17 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and several micronutrients (B-vitamins, vitamin D, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169403 (Mushrooms, oyster, raw) to supplement HPB data which does not report these sub-fractions. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1: these values are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw oyster mushroom as of 2026-04-16. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): oyster mushrooms are a raw plant-based food with no trans fatty acids by nature. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): oyster mushrooms are a fungal food with no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 169403). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): oyster mushrooms do not contain bioavailable vitamin B12. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for non-starchy mushrooms based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for Pleurotus ostreatus is available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301665 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as defensible anchors; SNOMED CT code 256319004 refers to Pleurotus ostreatus as a substance. These are cited in good faith from published ontology resources; if any code has been retired or reassigned, the gap note should be updated accordingly.
